invalid.description: Check that you picked the right file and that the table is filled in correctly.
invalid.title: File not recognized

tooManyRows.description: More than 250 rows detected. Beyond this limit, loading times grow significantly.
tooManyRows.title: Heads up

intro.lede: Stop reading rows and columns. GestaltBI surfaces the story your numbers are telling — the patterns, the exceptions, the openings — so you can decide better.
intro.pathUploadDesc: Drop a CSV or pick one from your machine. We turn your schema into dimensions and measures automatically.
intro.pathTemplateDesc: Pick the template that fits your business. Fill it in offline and bring it back here when you're ready to visualize.
intro.footTitle: Want a version built around your business?
intro.footText: We adapt GestaltBI to your sectors, KPIs, integrations — so the questions you ask each morning come pre-loaded.
intro.fileLoadedCorrectlyDesc: Click the button below to continue.
intro.title: Welcome

toolbar.tagline: the shape at the origin of meaning

change.title: Dynamic analysis of the change forces for a chosen analysis object
change.description: This analysis surfaces the effects of the main forces of change (costs, prices, volumes) and explains why the gross margin of a given analysis object shifted from one period to the next. For example, in one territory a rise in performance may be driven mostly by larger volumes; in another it may be the result of falling costs, or a favorable combination of both. Building this analysis through repeated database queries would be hard if not impossible. A dedicated model performs it automatically.

diff.title: Differential analysis
diff.description: Differential analysis represents the differences in performance. These can be differences for the same analysis object across two periods, or differences between two distinct objects within the same period. The value of differential analysis is that it shows only what's relevant — only what changed between the two units examined.

long.title: Longitudinal comparison of the same analysis object
long.description: This analysis lets you not only see the absolute values of performance — gross margin, prices, costs, volumes — for a freely-chosen object, but also compare how they evolve across two distinct time periods. Not isolated frames, but full motion.

longchange.title: Longitudinal comparison of the same analysis object
longchange.description: This analysis lets you not only see the absolute values of performance — gross margin, prices, costs, volumes — for a freely-chosen object, but also compare how they evolve across two distinct time periods. Not isolated frames, but full motion.

longdiff.title: Longitudinal comparison of the same analysis object
longdiff.description: This analysis lets you not only see the absolute values of performance — gross margin, prices, costs, volumes — for a freely-chosen object, but also compare how they evolve across two distinct time periods. Not isolated frames, but full motion.

point.title: Point analysis
point.description: GestaltBI's logic is to guide management toward understanding performance and how it changes. The product proposes a hierarchy of analyses to avoid what we call "analysis paralysis." Downstream of that hierarchy, each user pursues whichever specific investigations seem most useful in the moment. These are point inquiries — the classic territory of business intelligence: pick a specific object and analyze it, either to confirm an expectation or to discover something new to dig into.

sync.title: Synchronic comparison of performance across two different analysis objects
sync.description: With this analysis you can compare, within a chosen period, the performance of certain customers, products, or territories (or any combination of these) against another set. The more comparable the objects, the richer the learning. Pairing this with the change-forces analysis lets you understand what's actually causing improvements or declines. In the absence of reliable budget benchmarks, synchronic comparisons across different responsibility units can become one of the inputs for distributing incentives.

syncchange.title: Dynamic analysis of the change forces for a chosen analysis object
syncchange.description: This analysis surfaces the effects of the main forces of change (costs, prices, volumes) and explains why the gross margin of a given analysis object shifted from one period to the next. For example, in one territory a rise in performance may be driven mostly by larger volumes; in another it may be the result of falling costs, or a favorable combination of both. Building this analysis through repeated database queries would be hard if not impossible. A dedicated model performs it automatically.

syncdiff.title: Differential analysis
syncdiff.description: Differential analysis represents the differences in performance. These can be differences for the same analysis object across two periods, or differences between two distinct objects within the same period. The value of differential analysis is that it shows only what's relevant — only what changed between the two units examined.

terms.changeForces: An original aspect of GestaltBI is that it explains the variation in gross margin in terms of the main factors that drove it. Specifically, it decomposes the change in gross margin (whatever the object) into change in volumes sold, change in selling prices, and change in costs. This breakdown — absent from traditional BI tools — splits the problem rationally into its main components, giving a strong direction to interpretation and avoiding the so-called "analysis paralysis." The change forces are detected by a sophisticated algorithm that does not require — as traditional budget analyses do — predefined targets or budget values to compare actuals against.
terms.economicPerformance:
There are many economic performance indicators. The most meaningful ones relate to the firm's profitability — the ratio between gross margin in a given period and the average capital invested in that period — such as ROI, RONA, or ROE; for listed companies, share returns. Setting share returns aside, these indicators are typically measured annually. When the goal is a management system that fosters continuous learning and improvement, they are not ideal: (1) profitability does not change appreciably over short periods, and (2) it would be very hard, if not impossible, to trace the effects of daily operating actions to changes in profitability or share return. Infrequent feedback that cannot link actions to outcomes saps motivation and inhibits learning.
The principal short-period performance indicator is gross margin, or profit — the difference between all revenues and all matching costs of the period. In that case even a single month can be a meaningful interval. Some firms even produce monthly P&Ls. Shorter periods, however, are no longer compatible with measuring overall gross margin (no firm produces a daily P&L), because computing matching costs requires a virtual splitting of activities, which is demanding, expensive, and unreliable when the period is very short.
One synthetic economic indicator that retains its relevance even when used daily is the gross margin generated by product sales — revenues minus the cost of goods sold. By the matching principle, both values must be recorded simultaneously and contextually with each sale. Each transaction therefore updates the indicator, sometimes multiple times a day. Gross margin is the indicator GestaltBI chose as the apex of the pyramid from which to start the analysis process. It is the metric that best fits the principles of relevance, objectivity, and usability.
terms.analysisObjects: Management cannot limit itself to analyzing the firm's overall performance, even though that is certainly an important goal. It is the more targeted, specific analyses that reveal how to improve overall performance — what's holding back revenue growth, what's preventing cost containment, or both. An "object" of economic analysis is any entity for which management finds it useful to have economic information. Some objects are of permanent interest — departments, functions, divisions (so-called responsibility centers); often individual products are too. These are part of standard reporting, where firms systematically track costs and (when possible) revenues. But understanding requires specific attention that shifts over time with environmental and competitive factors. Management therefore needs a tool that flexibly defines any analysis object by composing variables — geographic area, customer, product, etc. A specific combination of these defines the analysis object in our system. An object is, in essence, a subset of the data.
terms.longitudinalSynchronic:
We speak of longitudinal analysis when the performance of the same "object" is compared over time. For example, analyzing the change in gross margin between February and March for a given customer group, geographic area, product combination, or any combination of these.
Synchronic analyses, by contrast, refer to a single period and therefore must compare two distinct "objects." Here it's essential to ensure the objects are comparable — i.e. that the performance comparison carries economic meaning. Comparing the January gross margin of a product to that of a customer would carry no economic meaning and would offer no learning. Comparing the gross margin of two different customers, on the other hand, can be a rich opportunity for reflection. Longitudinal and synchronic analyses are an alternative to the budget-style way of evaluating performance: not deviations from target values built on assumptions that grow unrealistic over time, but comparison — across time, or across comparable units of analysis.
terms.comparisonsDifferent: When one analysis object is compared with another, their differences can raise serious comparability issues. But if the analyst can account for those differences, external data provides a reference point for performance evaluation that becomes an important source for understanding the forces driving improvement or decline. Comparative analyses, by definition, contain more elements of difference than longitudinal ones (which refer to a single object), and so potentially enable richer learning, with reflections that wouldn't surface from a longitudinal view alone. Preliminary normalization can be very useful (for example, comparing two geographic areas with different numbers of customers and salespeople through ratios).
